title: Fix String.to_existing_atom used with attacker-seeded atom table
prompt:
The code below uses `String.to_existing_atom/1` — which LOOKS safe because it only converts strings that already exist in the atom table. But the atom table is populated at boot time by reading `config/roles.json`, which is writable by a content editor:

```elixir
# In application.ex:
def start(_, _) do
  {:ok, roles} = File.read("config/roles.json")
  roles
  |> Jason.decode!()
  |> Map.keys()
  |> Enum.each(&String.to_atom/1)  # pre-populate table

  # later in the supervision tree...
end

# In controller:
def show(conn, %{"role" => role}) do
  atom = String.to_existing_atom(role)
  render(conn, :show, role: atom)
end
```

An attacker with write access to `config/roles.json` can seed arbitrary strings into the atom table at boot, making `String.to_existing_atom/1` a useless guard. Rewrite the module so (a) the boot-time `String.to_atom/1` is replaced with a static compile-time map of known roles, (b) the controller uses the map for lookup (returns `:error` on unknown), (c) NO `String.to_atom` or `String.to_existing_atom` is called at runtime. Write the fix to `lib/my_app/roles.ex`.
tier_rationale: Hard — requires recognizing that to_existing_atom is only safe if the atom table itself is trustworthy. The fix requires eliminating runtime atomization entirely.
research_citation: Research dossier: 'String.to_existing_atom/1 used safely but the atom table is boot-populated from an attacker-writable config file, making the existing-atom check toothless.'
